coredata.dc:title: Theoretical investigation of red-shifted emission of graphitic boron doping in graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhaoshuang
Zhao, Fengjiao
Sui, Shuxin
Yang, Yang
Wen, Jiatao
Li, Haoji
Chen, Zhen
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are promising as luminescent materials in wide fields of applications. However, the narrow emission only at visible region restricts their further development. In this work, graphitic boron doping in GQDs have been theoretically investigated which yield red-shift emission of GQDs to NIR-MIR region. Calculated results reveal that both BC2 and BC3 bonding would narrow HOMO-LUMO gaps which achieve redshift of emission from 482 nm to 5619 nm. Moreover, for multiple B atoms doped GQDs containing both BC2 and BC3, these two bonding structures exhibit opposite trends: BC2 only dominates electron distribution at HOMO energy level while BC3 only dominates LUMO. Furthermore, geometrical distortion introduced by B-doping shows less impact on electronic and optical properties of GQD. This work reveals the mechanism of BC2 and BC3 bonding affecting electronic and optical properties and provides approaches to achieve red-shifted emission in NIR region of GQDs with considerable emission efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic boron doping
Graphene quantum dots
Near infrared luminescence
Theoretical investigation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Configuration of pristine GQD and schematic representation of three single graphitic boron doping sites. Grey atoms represent carbon, white atoms represent hydrogen.
Normalized emission spectra (a), emission efficiency (b), absorption spectra (c) and HOMO/LUMO energy levels (d) of calculated pristine GQD, A1, A2 and A3.

Top and side views of optimized configurations of ground-state (GS) and excited-state (ES) GQD, A1, A2 and A3, respectively and corresponding Molecular orbital diagrams of HOMO and LUMO in ground states. Note: grey atoms represent carbon, white atoms represent hydrogen and red atoms represent boron; dopped B atoms are labeled with red circles in HOMO and LUMO plots.

Density of state (DOS) of pristine GQD and A1-A3. TDOS and PDOS are the abbreviations of total DOS and partial DOS, respectively. Inserts in (b)(c)(d) are the details of DOS at LUMO level. Black and red dashed lines are laid at the positions of HOMO and LUMO levels, respectively.
Normalized emission (a), emission quantum yield (b), normalized absorbance (c) and HOMO/LUMO energy levels (d) of calculated B1-B6.

Top and side views of optimized configurations of ground-state (GS) and excited-state (ES) B1-B6 and corresponding molecular orbital diagrams of HOMO and LUMO in ground states. Note: grey atoms represent carbon, white atoms represent hydrogen and red atoms represent boron; dopped B atoms are labeled with red circles in HOMO and LUMO plots.
List of calculated absorption/emission peak wavelength in this work and comparison with other reports.
PDOS contribution of BC3 and BC2 bonding structures at HOMO and LUMO levels in all configurations.

head.ce:title: Theoretical investigation of red-shifted emission of graphitic boron doping in graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Advanced Science Facilities, Shenzhen, Guangdong 518107, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are promising as luminescent materials in wide fields of applications. However, the narrow emission only at visible region restricts their further development. In this work, graphitic boron doping in GQDs have been theoretically investigated which yield red-shift emission of GQDs to NIR-MIR region. Calculated results reveal that both BC2 and BC3 bonding would narrow HOMO-LUMO gaps which achieve redshift of emission from 482 nm to 5619 nm. Moreover, for multiple B atoms doped GQDs containing both BC2 and BC3, these two bonding structures exhibit opposite trends: BC2 only dominates electron distribution at HOMO energy level while BC3 only dominates LUMO. Furthermore, geometrical distortion introduced by B-doping shows less impact on electronic and optical properties of GQD. This work reveals the mechanism of BC2 and BC3 bonding affecting electronic and optical properties and provides approaches to achieve red-shifted emission in NIR region of GQDs with considerable emission efficiency.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a new kind of carbon-based zero-dimensional nanomaterials with graphitic structure which have a unique combination of several outstanding advantages including strong and tunable photoluminescence (PL), easy surface modification, high biocompatibility, high stability, low toxicity and low cost [1–5]. At the same time, GQD has the property of fluorescence quenching and can also be used as a sensing probe. Currently, precise identification of pesticides and metal ions (such as iron ions) in aqueous media has been achieved by synthesizing B and C atom co doped carbon quantum dots [6]. As a result, GQDs show great potential and have been widely used in solar cells [7,8], metal ion detection [9,10], photocatalyst [11,12]and especially, in biomedicine such as bioimaging [13,14], drug delivery [15,16], diagnosis of tumor [16,17], and etc. In such fields of applications, the detectable PL emission of GQDs is the fundamental property of bioimaging and disease diagnose.

In recent years, many strategies have been developed to regulate emission of GQDs and heteroatom doping has been revealed as an effective method via changing the electronic structure [18–22]. From previous reports, GQDs can be easily doped with various elements, such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus and boron. At present, N atom is the most commonly used for GQD doping since it has similar atomic radius with C atom and provides five valence electrons bonding with C atom. However, N-dopped GQDs usually yield blue-shifted PL emission which is dominated by multiple factors such as doping types and doping positions [23–25]. Considering that N atom has relatively larger electronegativity comparing with C atom which facilitates forming hydrogen bond and results in blue shift of PL emission, hence, to achieve red shift of GQDs PL emission, B atom is the top candidate for doping which has similar size to that of C atom and processes relatively small electronegativity. Many efforts have been made to experimentally achieve B doping into GQDs and reported researches have confirmed that B-dopped GQDs can be synthesized with relatively considerable doping efficiency [26,27]. Furthermore, it is encouraging that B-dopped GQDs exhibit PL emission in red and NIR region which broadens PL emission range comparing with GQDs and promotes the applications in bioimaging of GQDs [23,27].

However, for bioimaging, near infrared (NIR) emission, especially in NIR-II region (1500 nm–1700 nm) is preferred since the photon with relatively low energy can remarkedly reduce the molecular scattering, optical absorption of tissue which achieves deeper penetration depth, higher signal-to-background ratio (SBR) and better spatiotemporal resolution [28–30]. PL emission peaks of most reported GQDs were located in blue to green range [13,31]with the excitation of ultraviolet (UV) or blue light which severely restricted their applications, therefore, emission of GQDs in NIR region is in urgent demand. Meanwhile, mechanism study of B doping in GQDs regulating PL emission are limited and fundamental understandings of doped B atom affecting optical properties are still unclear.

In this work, doping of graphitic boron as BC3 and BC2 bonding structures has been investigated theoretically to reveal the key factors dominating the red-shifted emission of B-doped GQDs and aiming to find an optimal configuration for luminescence application. Here, subscript number is the number of carbon atoms around boron atom. As a result, BC3 bonding structure appears in doping of boron at inner skeleton of GQD and BC2 corresponds to boron doping into outer ‘layer’ of benzene rings. For these two doping structures, effect of both doping site and doping amount on geometrical configurations, electronic structures and optical properties have been systematically studies. The estimated results of spectra and HOMO/LUMO energy levels demonstrate that both BC2 and BC3 bonding structures can effectively narrow the HOMO-LUMO gap to achieve the redshift of absorption and emission peak of GQDs from visible region into NIR∼MIR region. Moreover, BC2 and BC3 bonding structures exhibit exact opposite influence on electronic properties of GQD when configurations of GQD with multiple B atoms doping containing both BC3 and BC2: BC2 only contributes to electron distribution at HOMO energy level while BC3 only dominates the electronic structures of LUMO. Furthermore, geometrical distortion reveals ignorable influence on electronic and optical properties of GQD. This paper addresses PL emission in NIR region of B-doped GQD from aspects of doping site and doping amount which provides fundamental understanding of B atom affecting optical properties and offers a strategy for NIR PL emission by B-doped CQD which promote the application of GQD in bioimaging.

Based on previous reports, size of GQDs is commonly within 10 nm and most synthesized GQDs range from 1 nm to 5 nm [32–35]. Herein, as shown in Fig. 1 , configuration of GQD which has lateral diameter of ∼2 nm with single-layer planar structure has been constructed which contains 34 benzene rings with total atoms of 126 (C96H30). To explore mechanism of boron-doping affecting radiative transitions of GQDs, both doping site and doping amount of boron atom have been investigated. For different doping sites, as shown in Fig. 1, single boron atom to be introduced into benzene rings inside GQD core structures which is BC3 bonding structure have been considered and are noted as A1, A2 and A3, respectively. Considering that experimental heteroatomic doping is more and more difficult when doping site gets closer to the core, three single doping sites distributed in outer three ‘layers’ benzene rings are considered in this work. For different doping numbers of boron atoms, 1–6 boron atoms have been doped into GQDs contain which are noted as B1, B2, B3, B4, B5 and B6, respectively.

Given that the number of total atoms of constructed GQD in this work is 126 and atomic numbers of all elements in GQD are below six, density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) methods with functional of B3LYP and basis set of 6-31G are suitable to yield credible simulation results with relatively affordable amounts of computations [36–40]. Gaussian 09 programs were used for configuration optimization and energy calculation of all B-GQDs and GQDs at both ground state (GS) and excited state (ES) [41]. All simulations in this work have been done in vacuum. The figures of density of states were obtained by Multiwfn [42].

The emission spectra for all configurations have been analyzed by optimizing the structures at the first excited state (S1) using TDDFT method. From Kasha's rule, transition from S1 to ground state (S0) typically accounts for the fluorescence emission. Especially, for B1 and B2 configurations, the oscillator strengths of S1 → S0 and S2 → S0 transitions yield zero indicating the emission is mainly attributed to the S3 → S0 transition which is the exception of Kasha's rule and this result is similar to the case of azulene in which the fluorescence comes from S2 → S0 transition. Moreover, emission efficiency is also involved in oscillator strength and coupling strength, herein the emission efficiency is obtained via area integration of every emission peak. For better comparation of B-doped GQD with pristine GQD, all peak area integrations obtained for all configurations have been normalized where the pristine GQD as 1.

The calculated emission spectra of pristine GQD and single boron atom dopped GQDs of A1-A3 are shown in Fig. 2a and in which all values of emission peaks have been normalized to 1. It is apparently that pristine GQD presents a sharp emission peak at visible light region centered @482 nm with full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 127 nm. From previous reports about experimental photoluminescence (PL) of GQDs which have similar sizes (∼2 nm of diameter) to our constructed GQDs in this work, the emission peak wavelength locates at ∼500 nm which supports our calculation result, moreover, this PL peak @∼500 nm are commonly ascribed to sp2 domine [31,34,43]. For A1-A3 structures, as shown in Fig. 2a, emission peaks show significant redshift comparing with pristine GQD locating at NIR region which are 1364 nm, 1171 nm and 1349 nm with FWHM of 1158 nm, 821 nm and 1129 nm for A1, A2 and A3, respectively. To better investigate single boron doping affect radiative transition processes, emission efficiencies of GQD, A1, A2 and A3 have been compared and shown in Fig. 2b (Emission efficiency of GQD has been normalized to 1 and calculation method is detailed in SI2). It can be found that single boron doping at such three sites decreases emission efficiency comparing with pristine GQD which from 1.00 (GQD) to 0.56 (A1), 0.15 (A2) and 0.49 (A3), respectively. Considering that FWHM of A1-A3 has been enlarged compared with pristine GQD and emission efficiency depends on emission peak area, as a result, compared with pristine GQD, emission efficiency at specific wavelength of A1-A3 is still low and A2 configuration presents better emission properties of relatively narrower emission peak and higher emission efficiency comparing with A1 and A3. The unnormalized emission spectra of GQD, A1-A3 are shown in Fig. S1.

To better understand the effect of energy levels by single boron atom doping into GQD, normalized absorption spectra of pristine GQD and A1-A3 are illustrated in Fig. 2c. Moreover, Fig. S2 of A1-A3 calculated absorption spectra show identical trend with emission spectra and such large red shift and reduced absorption of A1-A3 structures compared with pristine GQD verifies electron-deficient graphitic boron doping into graphene lattice would markedly narrow band gap of GQD which results in redshift of spectra and reducing sp2 clusters which lead to weakened absorption intensity [21,23,44,45]. Moreover, to further investigate the effect of different B doping sites on GQD's energy levels, the band gap between the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) of pristine GQD and A1-A3 are shown in Fig. 2d. Comparing with pristine GQD, A1-A3 configurations all exhibit the significant decrease of band gap between HOMO and LUMO from 2.70 eV (GQD) to 1.22 eV (A1), 1.12 eV (A2) and 1.09 eV (A3) which accounts for notable red shift of absorption spectra. This trend is consistent with previous reports where boron atom doping would lead to red shift of absorption spectra which corresponds to the narrowing of HOMO-LUMO gap [22,45]. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2d, HOMO energy levels of A1-A3 almost keep unchanged comparing with pristine GQD. Therefore, the narrowing of HOMO-LUMO gap of A1-A3 mainly results from the reduction of LUMO energy levels which further reveals that the electron-deficient graphitic boron (BC3) configuration plays a key role in regulating LUMO energy levels of GQD.

The optimized configurations of both ground-state (GS) and excited-state (ES) of pristine GQD and A1-A3 are illustrated in Fig. 3 . At ground state, all configurations show similar flat structures which indicates graphitic boron doping with the forming of BC3 bonding barely changes GQD's original geometrical structure. However, at excited state, A1 presents obvious distortion of planar structure and other configurations of GQD, A2 and A3 remain flat structures after excitation. This result suggests that graphitic boron doping at edge site leads to geometrical distortion of GQD while BC3 at core site inside GQD has ignorable impact on configuration of GQD. Furthermore, to investigate graphitic boron affecting electron distribution of GQD, the HOMO and LUMO orbitals of pristine GQD and A1-A3 are also plotted in Fig. 3. For pristine GQD, delocalized π bond distributes among the whole face of GQD plane for both HOMO and LUMO. For A1-A3, both HOMO and LUMO maintain delocalized π bond among the surface of carbon skeleton, however, electron distribution of HOMO and LUMO shows different trend. As shown in Fig. 3, the HOMO of A1-A3 present similar electron distribution comparing with GQD and no bonded electron cloud locating at B atoms. However, the LUMO of A1-A3 shows significant aggregation of electron near the B atoms. It illustrates that graphitic boron would markedly tuning the electronic structure of GQD which is achieved by regulating the LUMO instead of HOMO. This result further supports the results of absorption spectra and energy levels of HOMO/LUMO. It is worth noting that the geometry distortion caused by graphitic boron doping yields less effect on electronic structures of GQD which influences energy level distribution and determines the optical properties.

Density of state (DOS) results can further reveal the electronic structures and the contribution of different bonding structures. To better compare and confirm the contributions of domain carbon atom in GQD and doped boron atom in A1-A3 to total DOS (TDOS), both TDOS of all configurations and partial DOS (PDOS) of carbon atom for pristine GQD and BC3 for A1-A3 have been plotted in Fig. 4 . For pristine GQD, PDOS of C atoms is highly consistent with TDOS especially at range between HOMO and LUMO which indicates that carbon atom orbitals are predominant in electronic structures of pristine GQD. For A1-A3, comparing with pristine GQD, boron atom doping apparently changes distribution of TDOS. Particularly, at energy level of HOMO, PDOS of BC3 for A1-A3 approach zero implies that B doping has no effect on DOS at HOMO and C atoms orbitals would still domain the HOMO energy levels. This supports the results of unchanged HOMO energy levels of A1-A3 comparing with the pristine GQD. However, PDOS of BC3 present considerable proportion of TDOS at energy level of LUMO and this indicates that boron atoms play an important role in changing electronic structure to reform LUMO which is consistent with previous results of markedly changed LUMO energy levels of A1-A3. Furthermore, TDOSs of A1-A3 exhibit different distributions near HOMO energy levels. Among these three configurations, TDOS of A2 yields a relatively narrower peak with less overlapping at HOMO and TDOSs of A1 and A3 show stronger intensity at energy level just below HOMO. This may explain the spectral result which A2 has blue-shifted and narrower absorption peak comparing with A1 and A3.

Multiple heteroatoms doping (B1-B6) into GQD has been also investigated and the calculated emission spectra after normalization of the pristine GQD and B1-B6 are shown in Fig. 5a. Compared with pristine GQD (em@482 nm), it is evident that emission peaks of B1-B6 all exhibit significant redshift which locate at 1373 nm (B1), 1459 nm (B2), 3970 nm (B3), 2940 nm (B4), 3340 nm (B5), 5619 nm (B6) with FWHM of 1219 nm, 1352 nm, 26,730 nm, 10,863 nm, 19,953 nm and 61,553 nm for B1-B6, respectively. Comparing with B3-B6, B1 and B2 have much sharper and high-energy emission. Moreover, emission efficiency yields 1.00, 1.13, 1.73, 0.30, 1.58, 0.17 and 0.60 for the pristine GQD, B1, B2, B3, B4, B5 and B6, respectively. It can be found that B1, B2 and B4 reveal excellent performance in emission efficiency comparing with the pristine GQD. Considering that the emission efficiency depends on intensity and FWHM, the unnormalized emission spectra have been shown in Fig. S3 to provide an overview of emission properties of GQD and B1-B6.

To investigate the doping amount and bonding structure affecting electronic structures and optical properties of GQD, normalized absorption spectra of pristine GQD and B1-B6 are illustrated in Fig. 5c. Alike, absorption peak of B1-B6 show red shift and reduced absorption comparing with the pristine GQD. (Unnormalized absorption spectra can be found in Fig. S4.) This further supports that both BC3 and BC2 boding could both reduce band gap of GQD and lead to significant redshift of spectral peaks. As shown in Fig. 5d, the calculated HOMO and LUMO energy levels reveal that comparing with pristine GQD, B1-B6 configurations all show markedly decrease of HOMO-LUMO gap from 2.70 eV (GQD) to 1.19 eV (B1), 0.34 eV (B2), 1.04 eV (B3), 0.66 eV (B4), 0.62 eV (B5) and 0.65 eV (B6) which corresponds to the redshift of absorption spectra. As shown in Fig. 6 , for configurations of B1 and B2 whose doped graphitic boron is BC2 bonding, it is worth noting that comparing with pristine GQD, both HOMO and LUMO energy levels have been changed. This result indicates that BC2 bonding plays an important role in tuning both HOMO and LUMO energy levels whereas BC3 bonding tunes only LUMO energy level which has been above-discussed.

Based on result of A1-A3, the change of steric configuration caused by single boron atom doping as BC3 structure introduces ignorable impact on electronic structures of GQD. To further figure out the influence of geometry change arises from different amount and bonding structure of boron doping, the optimized GS and ES configurations of B1-B6 have been calculated and illustrated in Fig. 6. At ground state, B5 remains flat structure and all other configurations present plane torsion. At excite state, B5 keeps the plane structure, B3 returns flat as the pristine GQD and other configurations remains torsional structures as ground-state configurations. This result indicates that BC2 bonding and BC3 bonding at edge site would lead to plane distortion and BC3 bonding at core site produces no change to GQD configuration. This conclusion is consistent with that of A1-A3 which has been discussed above. Moreover, combing with the amount and doing site of BC2 and BC3 in each configuration of B1-B6, the estimated result in this work demonstrates that geometrical change caused by graphitic doping has no influence on electronic structures of GQD. For luminescence applications, the photophysical properties of B2 exhibits excellent characters with emission peak at 1459 nm which locates in NIR region and the highest emission efficiency of 1.7 times comparing with that of the pristine GQD. As a result, B2 can be considered as a promising candidate of B-doped GQDs used in luminescence applications. For better comparison, calculated absorption and emission peak wavelength in this work with previously theoretical and experimental results are listed in Table 1 .

Furthermore, HOMO and LUMO orbitals of B1-B6 are also exhibited in Fig. 6. For B1 with one BC2 bonding, HOMO presents typical delocalized π bond distributes among the whole face of carbon skeleton and LUMO shows highly centralized electron distribution around BC2 bonding which accounts for the barely changed HOMO but markedly reduced LUMO energy levels of B1. However, for B2 with two BC2 bonding symmetrically locating at both sides of GQD, both HOMO and LUMO yield significant variation with localized π bond distributing of carbon atoms at lower and middle parts. Meanwhile, the delocalized π bond focused on the location near boron atoms. This demonstrates that BC2 bonding could effectively tune electronic structures of GQD and regulate both HOMO and LUMO. This conclusion further supports spectral and HOMO/LUMO energy level results in Fig. 5. Moreover, the increase amount of BC2 bonding leads to the increase of delocalized π electron distribution at boron atom and changing the π bond of carbon atoms from delocalized to localized. Combing with spectral results in Fig. 5, the estimated result indicates that BC2 bonding would promote efficiency of radiative transitions by effectively tuning the electron distribution and molecular orbital of GQD. For B3 with one BC2 and two BC3 bonding, electron distribution as delocalized π bond for both HOMO and LUMO locates at BC3 area instead of BC2 corresponds to lower emission efficiency of B3 which further verifies the proposed mechanism of BC2 bonding on spectral and electronic properties of GQD. B4-B6 show the similar trends of BC2 bonding and BC3 bonding as with different influence on optical properties and electronic structures of GQD.

To further study the electronic structures derived from different bonding structures with different amount of boron atoms, DOS of B1-B6 have been calculated and results are shown in Fig. S5. For B1 with single BC2 bonding in Fig. S5(a), it is obvious that PDOS of BC2 contributes to DOS at LUMO level instead of HOMO. This result is identical to that of A1-A3 with single BC bonding. For multiple BC2 bonding structures of B2 (2BC2), DOS at both HOMO and LUMO levels yields proportion of BC2. Similarly, for multiple BC3 bonding structures of B6 (6BC3), DOS show considerable contributions at both HOMO and LUMO levels. For other configurations containing both BC2 and BC3 boding which are B3 (1BC2 + 2 BC3), B4 (2BC2 + 2 BC3) and B5 (1BC2 + 4 BC3), PDOS of BC2 show contribution to DOS at HOMO instead of LUMO levels. To better compare the effect of PDOS of BC2 and BC3 bonding, PDOS contributions of BC3 and BC2 bonding at HOMO and LUMO levels in all configurations have been summarized in Table 2 . It can be concluded that for GQD which contains single BC3 or BC2, DOS at only LUMO level shows proportion of the corresponding B bond structures, however, for GQD which has multiple doping sites with single kind of bonding structure, DOS at both HOMO and LUMO levels yields contributions. Moreover, it is interesting that for GQDs which have both BC3 and BC2 structures with different amount, BC3 bonding only contributes to DOS at the LUMO level instead of HOMO level, while BC2 bonding exhibits opposite result which it only contributes to DOS at the HOMO level instead of LUMO level. This result reveals mechanism of BC2 and BC3 bonding structures with different doping component proportions on the electronic structures of GQD. Furthermore, it is obvious that broader TDOS distributions at LUMO energy level of B3-B6 show up comparing with the pristine GQD and A1-A3 which further supports the enlarged FWHM emission peaks of B3-B6.

Considering that carbon-based QDs materials have abundant functional groups like carbonyl groups, hydroxyl and especially, BH bonding, which has been investigated in our work, solvent effects are significant since hydrogen bonds are inevitable when they are stored in water, methanol, etc. From previous reports, hydrogen bonds between carbon-based quantum dots and solvents would enhance energy resonance transfer and finally lead to redshift of PL. [50–52] Therefore, luminescence of boron-doped GQDs in this work is possibly yield stronger redshift in experimental emission spectra comparing with the calculated emission spectra. Furthermore, the stability of configurations has been considered. The frequencies and binding energies of all boron-doped configurations have been calculated and the results are listed in Table S2. It can be found that, all configurations yield positive values of frequencies which suggests their stable structures except B5. For binding energy results, all configurations shows positive values from 7.39 eV to 7.56 eV which suggest that all configurations have a relatively configurations. These results indicate that all configurations are relatively stable which could be synthesized experimentally whereas B5 may not stable enough in reality.

In this work, the optical properties, geometrical configurations and electronic structures of boron-doped GQDs with BC2 and BC3 bonding structures have been investigated. It can be concluded that both BC2 and BC3 bonding structures can effectively tune electronic properties of GQD via narrowing the HOMO-LUMO gaps which yield absorption and emission with lower energy and this corresponds to the red-shift of absorption and emission peak from visible region to NIR-MIR region. Moreover, BC2 and BC3 bonding structures exhibit different contributions to electronic structure change in GQD at various doping situations: (1) for GQD with single B atom doping as BC3 or BC2, B bond structure alters electron distribution at only LUMO level; (2) for GQD with multiple B atoms doping with single kind of bonding structure (BC3 or BC2), electronic structures of both HOMO and LUMO have been changed; (3) for GQD with multiple B atoms doping with mixed bonding structures of BC3 and BC2, BC2 only dominates the electronic properties of HOMO while BC3 only dominates the electronic structures of LUMO. Furthermore, for all configurations in this work, geometrical distortion introduced by boron doping shows ignorable impact on electronic and optical properties of GQD. Combing electronic structures with spectral results, BC2 bonding produces higher emission efficiency with relatively broader emission peak and BC3 bonding leads to lower emission efficiency with relatively narrower emission peak. Therefore, BC2 bonding doping of GQD matches the demands of NIR-MIR emission with larger emission range like wide IR spectrum detection and BC3 bonding doping is more fit for applications require NIR emission at specific wavelength. For GQD materials, graphitic boron doping is still difficult comparing with decorating boron atom at edge sites of GQD by replacing hydrogen atom and more experimental spectrum results are still lacking for further verification.
Authors declare no extra statement about this manuscript.

Zhaoshuang Liu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Fengjiao Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Shuxin Sui: Data curation. Yang Yang: Supervision, Software. Jiatao Wen: Formal analysis. Haoji Li: Formal analysis. Zhen Chen: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant NO. 3132023197) and open fund of the state key laboratory of molecular reaction dynamics in DICP, CAS.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.110815.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence and sensing applications of graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots: a review
Chemistry-an Asian Journal

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zheng, P.; Wu, N. Q., Fluorescence and Sensing Applications of Graphene Oxide and Graphene Quantum Dots: A Review. Chemistry-an Asian Journal 2017, 12 (18), 2343-2353.
